question_text: Which statement correctly explains why average speed is often different from instantaneous speed?

options.label: A
options.text: Because objects always travel at a constant speed

options.label: B
options.text: Because objects speed up, slow down, or stop during a journey

options.label: C
options.text: Because time is measured differently in different places

options.label: D
options.text: Because distance cannot be measured accurately

correct_answer: Because objects speed up, slow down, or stop during a journey
explanation: Average speed is calculated by dividing the total distance travelled by the total time taken for the whole journey. Instantaneous speed is the speed at one specific moment in time. In real journeys, objects rarely travel at constant speed - they accelerate, decelerate, or stop at various points. This variation in speed means the average speed is usually different from the speed at any particular instant.
